# Questions in Livestream 234

Live Stream #234: The Nephilim Are Coming For You

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=687" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:27</a> | Is Jesus or Mary the Ark of the Covenant in typology? | He rejects the common Marian argument: God met Israel above the ark, not inside it, so the ark works better as a throne image than as a vessel-for-Mary analogy. | He says the Marian typology depends on treating the ark as a container for God, but in the biblical description the glory is enthroned above the ark between the cherubim. Because the divine presence is not inside the box, he thinks the comparison collapses and that the ark functions more naturally as a throne or footstool image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=866" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:26</a> | Didn't the ark also have Aaron's staff in it? | He says no: the tablets were in the ark itself, while items like Aaron's staff and the manna jar were more likely kept in a separate vessel before it. | He distinguishes the ark itself from another accompanying container. The covenant tablets belonged inside the ark, while Aaron's staff and the manna jar were placed before it, probably in a smaller reliquary-like vessel associated with the ark but not contained in it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=1243" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:43</a> | What is the context of 1 Corinthians 14:34-35 on women? | He reads it as a rule against disorderly interruption or unauthorized preaching in worship, not as a ban on women ever speaking in church. | He rejects an absolute-silence reading because ordinary church life and other New Testament passages assume women can speak. In context, he treats Paul's command as regulating disruptive speech, questions, or teaching claims in a worship setting where order and proper authority are the issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=1612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:52</a> | What does "we shall judge angels" mean in 1 Corinthians 6:3? | He sketches several views, especially judging angels' service or judging church "messengers" such as bishops, and he treats the "judging fallen angels" view as less likely. | He roots the phrase in Paul's argument that Christians should handle disputes within the church rather than drag one another before pagan courts. He then weighs possible meanings of "angels," including heavenly beings, angelic service, or human messengers, and leans away from making the verse mainly about believers sentencing fallen angels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=2031" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:51</a> | What were the tumors in 1 Samuel 6:5? | He says they were some kind of painful growths or tumors, sometimes rendered as hemorrhoids, but the exact medical identification is uncertain. | He identifies the episode as the Philistines suffering after mishandling the ark. The affliction is described as tumors, with some translations and traditions making them hemorrhoids, but he keeps the medical details tentative and focuses on them as a painful divine judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=2176" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:16</a> | What is the significance of the cows in 1 Samuel 6:7 never having had a yoke? | He says that kept them ritually pure by ensuring they had not previously hauled corpses or served in ways associated with impurity. | He explains that a yoke could imply prior work pulling a wagon, and wagons were used in funerary contexts to carry the dead. Choosing milk cows that had never been yoked helped avoid any suspicion that the animals or cart had been involved with corpse impurity before transporting the ark. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=2427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:27</a> | If impurity transfers but holiness does not, would the later ox cart have defiled the ark? | He says no: the ark itself would not be defiled, but using an impure cart would still offend God and bring judgment. | He distinguishes defiling the ark from offending God by improper handling. The ark is made of ritually pure materials and is not something an ordinary impurity can simply contaminate, but treating it with an improper or impure conveyance still violates its holiness and provokes divine wrath. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=2606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:26</a> | Is Beth-shemesh a city dedicated to the god Shemesh? | He says the place-name does come from Shemesh, the sun god, even if the term later also carried a more ordinary "sun" sense. | He reads Beth-shemesh as "house of Shemesh" and connects the name to the Mesopotamian sun deity. He notes that such pagan divine names appear in Israelite geography and personal names, while allowing that "shemesh" can also function as an ordinary word for the sun. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=3053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:53</a> | What were the elements of the Seder meal in Christ's time, and what did he likely eat before the betrayal? | He describes an early Seder with the cups, lamb, unleavened flatbread, bitter herbs, eggs, and a sweet mixture, while noting later Jewish practice changed some details. | He says the Passover meal had already developed recognizable ritual elements by the first century, including multiple cups and the Elijah cup. He lists lamb, unleavened bread, bitter herbs, eggs, and a sweet paste-like component, but cautions that later Seder custom is not identical to the meal as practiced in Jesus' day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=3431" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:11</a> | Does `hamats levan` refer to acidity or rising action, and is baking soda included? | He treats it mainly as a leavening issue in food: baking soda counts for Passover food use even though it is not yeast. | He separates food use from other uses of the term. For Passover food, he treats the concern as yeast or leavening action, so baking soda would be inappropriate in unleavened bread even though it is chemically different from yeast. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=3680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:20</a> | So baking soda is `hamats` for food but not for cleaning? | Yes. He says it matters in the food sense for Passover, but it is not something you must purge from the kitchen the way yeast or mold is. | He affirms the distinction directly: baking soda can be used for cleaning without a Passover problem. It only becomes relevant as `hamats` in the ritual food context during the seven days of Passover, where its leavening function matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=3770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:50</a> | What kind of wine would first-century Jews and Christians have drunk, and what modern wine is closest to it? | He says it was wild-yeast wine that was often rough or poor quality; his modern comparison is cheap low-end red wine. | He explains that ancient wine usually fermented from yeasts naturally present on grape skins rather than from carefully selected strains. Because the process was less controlled, he expects much first-century wine to have been rough, inconsistent, and closer to cheap, low-end red wine than to modern refined bottles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=3931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:31</a> | Could most fruits theoretically be turned into alcohol? | Yes. He says many fruits can ferment into cider or wine under the right conditions. | He says fruit generally has sugar and either carries yeast naturally or can pick it up from the air. He gives examples such as berries, plums, cherries, peaches, apricots, citrus, and pineapple, while noting that different fruits ferment more easily than others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=4450" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:10</a> | Can you make tomatoes into wine? | Yes, but he strongly advises against it and describes it as disgusting. | He says tomato wine is possible but treats it as an extremely bad idea, comparing it to prison wine. He adds that tomato fermentation can be stopped and sweetened into something like ketchup, but fully fermented tomato wine is presented as foul and regrettable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=4686" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:06</a> | Can Christians between churches take communion at home with fellow displaced believers, and how should they do it reverently? | He says yes, provided it is done reverently, with other believers in community, and not as a solitary act. | He says communion must be done as an act of Christian community, invoking the principle of two or three gathered together. He cautions against flippancy and notes that some denominations restrict administration to ordained ministers, but his answer allows it when believers follow Scripture reverently together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5391" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:51</a> | Why was the ark not returned to Shiloh in 1 Samuel 7:2, and why Kiriath-jearim instead? | He says Beth-shemesh had proved irresponsible, and Shiloh itself had likely already been destroyed. | He says Beth-shemesh was not a good place to keep the ark because its people had behaved irresponsibly with it. Shiloh was also not a real option because, although the Bible does not narrate every detail there, he thinks Shiloh had been destroyed after the ark left and had nothing left to receive it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:27</a> | But if Yahweh was with the ark, why gather at Mizpah in 1 Samuel 7:6? | He says confession and prayer did not require standing before the ark; Israel could repent and seek God elsewhere. | He acknowledges that the Shekinah glory is associated with the ark, but says Israel did not have to go to Kiriath-jearim for confession, fasting, and prayer. Repentance could happen at Mizpah or another suitable sacred place without standing physically before the ark or tabernacle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:20</a> | Wasn't it wrong for Samuel to build an altar in 1 Samuel 7:17? | He says pre-Temple Israel could still build altars; the ban on local altars came only after worship was centralized at the Temple. | He places Samuel before the Solomonic Temple, when Israelites still used multiple altars and high places for Yahweh worship. The later problem comes after worship is centralized, but before that point an altar could be legitimate if it followed the required rules. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5719" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:19</a> | Why did those early altars have to be made of natural stone and without a staircase? | He says the stones were to remain God's work rather than human-crafted, and the lack of stairs prevented exposing nakedness at the altar. | He explains the natural-stone rule as a way of avoiding an altar manufactured by human hands: God made the stones. The no-stair or no-ramp rule is tied to modesty, since ancient clothing made ascent before the altar a potential exposure problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:10</a> | Is 1 Samuel 9:9 a lexical gloss from a later scribe or the original author writing later? | He calls it a clear scribal gloss inserted to explain that "seer" was an older term for "prophet." | He reads the verse's explanation of "seer" and "prophet" as an inserted clarification for later readers. Since the note pauses the narrative to explain older vocabulary, he treats it as a straightforward scribal gloss rather than part of the original scene. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=5990" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:50</a> | How should the resurrection of the body be understood? Is God recreating it from nothing or restoring what remains? | He says the resurrection is certain but the mechanics are not revealed; Christians know only that the risen body is embodied, glorified, and immortal. | He says the mechanics of resurrection are a divine secret and any detailed account is speculative. He allows that where remains exist they may be reassembled or restored, but his main point is that God brings the dead back in embodied, glorified, immortal life regardless of how the process works. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=6208" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:28</a> | In Matthew 7:21, is Jesus warning about people trying to earn heaven by works or about carnal Christians? | He says the passage targets people who do impressive religious acts without truly knowing Christ or living under his authority. | He emphasizes that the people in the passage call Jesus Lord and even claim prophecy, exorcism, and miracles. The warning, as he reads it, is not about anxious believers lacking spectacular deeds but about people whose religious actions are not joined to knowing Christ and doing the Father's will. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=6803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:23</a> | Does Eastern Orthodoxy read Matthew 16:18 the same way Catholic polemics do? | No. He says the Orthodox reading treats the rock as Christ rather than Peter in the specifically Catholic sense. | He contrasts the Catholic polemical reading, which ties the rock to Peter, with the Orthodox interpretation he describes. In his account, the Orthodox distinguish Peter from the larger rock and identify the rock on which the church is built as Jesus Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=6891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:51</a> | Why does God leave some issues nebulous, such as the origin of the soul or Genesis 6? | He says Scripture is meant to be wrestled with, and some ambiguities force spiritual growth, dependence on the Holy Spirit, and humility about non-central details. | He says Scripture is not meant to serve every answer on a platter. The difficulty and depth of ambiguous passages require readers to wrestle, learn, and grow, and he suggests that sometimes believers are not given everything because they are not ready or because the struggle itself is spiritually useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=7149" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:09</a> | What are your thoughts on cremation and its implications for the resurrected body? | He says cremation does not prevent bodily resurrection; God can restore a person from ashes as easily as from bones. | He notes that some Christian traditions historically opposed cremation because of its symbolism, but says that concern has largely faded. For resurrection, he sees no real barrier: God can restore a body from ashes, bones, or other remains, and he even mentions Israelite kings in connection with cremation practices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=7671" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:51</a> | Does 1 Samuel 9:14 show Samuel offering sacrifices at a high place? | Yes. He says that was normal Yahweh worship before Solomon's Temple centralized sacrifice. | He reads the passage as showing Samuel going to a high place where the people wait for him to bless the sacrifice before eating. He defines a high place here as a holy site for Yahweh worship and says this was normal before the Jerusalem Temple centralized sacrificial practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=7869" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:09</a> | Why is Samuel performing sacrifices in 1 Samuel 10:8, and how does being an honorary Levite work? | He says Samuel functioned as a divinely approved priestly substitute because Eli's house had no eligible priest, and later tradition honorarily associates his line with Levi. | He says the collapse of Eli's house left no proper priestly figure to do the work, so God used Samuel to intercede and handle sacrifices in a priestly role. He treats Samuel as a divinely authorized substitute, with later tradition resolving the problem by associating him honorarily with Levi. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=8142" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:42</a> | What is Samuel writing in 1 Samuel 10:25? | He takes it as the king's legal ordinances or copy of the law that Saul was supposed to have and read. | He identifies the writing as the ordinances of the kingdom, effectively the law the king was required to know. In his reading, Samuel is giving Saul a copy or written form of the law so the king can read it regularly and rule justly under God's commands. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=8296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:16</a> | If Genesis 6 only says the Nephilim were around, does that mean they were not offspring of the sons of God? | He rejects that reading and argues the Nephilim are still connected to the sons of God, though he treats them as human rather than angelic hybrids. | He says the Nephilim are men, not demons or angelic hybrids, but still arise from the Genesis 6 offense. In his view, "sons of God" refers to a priestly class and "daughters of Adam" to a wrong religious line, so the issue is covenantal or vow-breaking intermarriage rather than supernatural reproduction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=8852" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:32</a> | Assuming the story is authentic, what do you think Jesus wrote on the ground in John 8:6-9? | He says the story is not part of original John, so any answer about what Jesus wrote is speculative. | He says textual criticism shows the adulterous-woman pericope was added later and is not part of the original text of John. Because the passage itself is not original and the wording never says what Jesus wrote, he refuses to assign a definite answer beyond possible speculation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:12</a> | Why do we keep the adulterous-woman story in the Bible if it is not authentic to John's text? | He says tradition is the main reason, especially its long presence in familiar printed Bibles. | He says the passage remains because readers are attached to traditional Bibles, especially the King James tradition, and would react strongly if familiar material were removed. He compares that impulse to other traditional renderings that persist even when scholars think a different reading is better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:03</a> | Why is the adulterous-woman scenario a trap for Jesus? | He says either answer exposes him: releasing her looks lawless, while condemning her ignores the missing male offender and still creates legal trouble. | He explains that if Jesus releases the woman, opponents can accuse him of ignoring the law, but if he condemns her, the case is still legally compromised because the man is absent. By telling the sinless one to cast the first stone, Jesus shifts the burden back onto the accusers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9133" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:13</a> | Has anyone produced an updated Bible that removes later additions like this? | He says he does not know of one and suggests translators are usually too risk-averse to remove familiar traditional material. | He says translators and publishers tend to avoid the backlash that would come from removing familiar glosses, additions, or traditional readings. Even where a gloss is obvious or awkward, he thinks the practical risk of public controversy keeps it in printed Bibles. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:27</a> | What scriptures shed light on the competence, incompetence, or intelligence of angels? | He answers broadly that angels and demons vary widely in power and competence, much as humans do, and often operate cooperatively rather than as isolated actors. | He says there is huge diversity among spirit beings: some are good, some evil, some wiser or stronger than others. He points to the fall of some angels as evidence that not all are wise, and he resists treating "angel" as automatically meaning competent, intelligent, or morally sound. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9799" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:19</a> | What do you think of C. S. Lewis's *Screwtape Letters* and its picture of demons? | He calls it a fun read with some useful flavor, but not an especially accurate model of how demons actually operate. | He says Lewis's picture of demons undermining one another is more like an analogy for human behavior than a reliable demonology. He still finds the book enjoyable and somewhat insightful, but treats much of its demon dynamics as literary flavor rather than an accurate operational model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:04</a> | Didn't C. S. Lewis talk about demonic experiences while writing *The Screwtape Letters*? | He says much of the book is fictionalized; it is not his best model of demons, though it can be a useful starting point for someone new to the topic. | He says that even if Lewis connected the writing process with demonic experience, the work itself is heavily fictionalized. He would not use it as the best representation of demons, though he grants that it can be a useful beginner's entry point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=9948" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:48</a> | Why are sacrifices being made away from the ark in 1 Samuel 11:15? | He says most sacrifices did not require the ark's presence; only the national Day of Atonement rite was uniquely tied to it. | He says almost all sacrifices could be offered without the ark nearby, including peace offerings such as those in Gilgal. The one sacrifice he singles out as ark-dependent is the national Yom Kippur rite, so ordinary sacrifices away from the ark are not surprising. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=10134" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:48:54</a> | How would it be sin for Samuel not to pray for the people in 1 Samuel 12:23? | He says Samuel was acting in an intercessory priestly role, so refusing to pray would have been dereliction of duty. | He says Samuel had been called to intercede for Israel, especially because Eli's house had failed to provide a proper priestly mediator. Since praying for the people was part of the high-priestly role Samuel was filling, ceasing to pray would mean refusing his assigned duty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=10285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:25</a> | Joseph lived 110 years, Moses 120, Joseph was sold for 20 pieces of silver, and Jesus for 30. Is that showing Jesus is greater than Joseph? | He thinks the thirty pieces likely carry some further cultural significance, but he could not recall it and leaves the answer incomplete. | He says Joseph's twenty pieces may reflect a wholesale slave price, but he is not sure how that compares to the price in Jesus' time. He suspects the thirty pieces of silver has additional significance beyond inflation or simple comparison, but explicitly says he cannot remember the detail and passes on a firm answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=10485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:45</a> | Was Gideon's father a priest of Baal since the family had an altar to Baal in Judges 6:24-25? | He says the text is ambiguous: the altar is tied to Gideon's father or ancestor, but it does not clearly prove he served as Baal's priest. | He notes that the altar is said to belong to Gideon's father, but he flags the ambiguity of "father" as possibly meaning an ancestor. The passage proves the household or family line was tied to the Baal altar, but it does not conclusively identify Gideon's living father as a priest of Baal. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=10792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:52</a> | What does "son of God" mean in the Bible? | He says the phrase can mean different things by context, including deity, angel, priestly human, or other relational titles. | He stresses that "son of God" is not a single-use phrase. Depending on context, it can refer to a lesser deity, an angel, a human priestly class, a divine son, or a relational title comparable to ancient phrases such as son or daughter of a deity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=10884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:24</a> | Did having a concubine count as adultery, or was it permitted? | He says it depends on the case; concubinage was broadly recognized in some settings, though priestly and covenant restrictions complicate it. | He says the Mosaic law mentions concubines only in passing, so the answer depends on status and circumstance. He treats concubines as recognized reproductive slaves in some contexts, notes special restrictions for Levites and covenant settings, and admits the subject is legally complex. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=11343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:03</a> | If we found a new authoritative gospel such as Thomas, would it become canon, and do oral traditions count as holy Scripture? | He says no: Thomas is historically treated as gnostic, and canon requires more than mere existence of a text or tradition. | He says the Gospel of Thomas could not become canon because it has historically been regarded as gnostic and lacks universal acceptance among the early churches and fathers. He treats canon as requiring broad apostolic and ecclesial recognition, not merely a text's discovery or an oral tradition's existence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=11461" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:01</a> | In 1 Samuel 16:12, what does "ruddy" or "reddish" mean about David? | He says it means David had a reddish or deeply tanned complexion from being outdoors as a shepherd. | He reads "ruddy" as a description of complexion rather than hair color or a symbolic trait. David is a shepherd spending time outside, so he takes the description as a reddish, sun-darkened, or tanned appearance along with the note about his eyes and looks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=11578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:58</a> | If we found a real gospel or letter written by Thomas, Paul, or another apostle, would that make it Scripture? | He says no. Apostolic authorship alone would not make a text canonical without wider church recognition. | He says even a genuine apostolic document would not automatically become Scripture, using Paul's other Corinthian letters as an example. For canonicity, he says a text must not only be apostolic but also received by the church as Scripture and consistent with the existing rule of faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=11793" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:33</a> | What if the Pope and Orthodox patriarchs all agreed a newly found apostolic text was legitimate? | He says canon is not theoretically closed, but a text would still need to meet a very high bar of authorship, consistency, and universal recognition. | He says such agreement is highly unlikely, but he does not treat the canon as theoretically impossible to add to. A new text would need provable apostolic authorship, consistency with the Old and New Testaments, and recognition across the whole church, including the apostolic sees. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=11997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:57</a> | Was there something special about becoming the king's son-in-law in 1 Samuel 18:23? | Yes. He says it effectively positioned someone for a ministerial role within the royal government. | He says becoming the king's son-in-law was not just a family honor; it brought someone into the royal household network. Since royal ministers and government figures were commonly blood relatives or relatives by marriage, David's concern about status makes sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=12138" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:22:18</a> | What is the household idol or teraphim in 1 Samuel 19:13? | He describes it as a fertility-type household idol, the sort of object later Israelites kept despite its obvious pagan associations. | He identifies the household idol as a teraphim and treats it as an object with fertility or household-cult associations. He notes that such items have been found in Israelite contexts and uses the scene to show that pagan or syncretistic household practices persisted among Israelites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=12338" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:38</a> | Is the Exodus "I am who I am" line a polemic taken from the Egyptian *Book of the Heavenly Cow*? | He says no: the Egyptian text and grammar do not support that claim, which he traces to bad fringe translation history. | He rejects the proposed Egyptian parallel emphatically, saying the *Book of the Heavenly Cow* does not say "I am who I am" and that the claimed grammar is wrong. He traces the idea to older fringe or conscious-community style claims rather than to sound Egyptology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=12946" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:46</a> | Hypothetically, if Nephilim were human rather than demonic, could they be saved through Christ's sacrifice? | He says yes in principle, because if they were human, salvation would still be possible. | He answers hypothetically that if the Nephilim are men rather than demons, then salvation through Christ is possible for them. He adds that nothing in the transcripted discussion rules out a Nephilim turning from the ancestor cult or false religion to the true faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=13066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:37:46</a> | Is the Sermon on the Mount passage around Matthew 5:37 about reparation? | He says no: the immediate context is vows, then retaliation and enemy-love, not reparations. | He initially cannot identify the question until the verse is clarified as Matthew 5:37. Once there, he says the surrounding argument concerns oath-taking, retaliation, and loving enemies, so he does not see reparations as the contextual point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=13526" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:26</a> | What is important about the prophesying scene in 1 Samuel 19:20-24? | He treats it as a display of overwhelming divine influence and clarity rather than as evidence of some unique office for Saul's men. | He reads the scene as repeated waves of Saul's messengers, and finally Saul himself, being overcome by the Spirit of God when they approach Samuel and the prophets. The point is God's overpowering intervention and control of the situation, not that Saul's agents hold a special prophetic office. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=13718" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:38</a> | What does "the shame of your mother's nakedness" mean in 1 Samuel 20:30? | He says Saul is reminding Jonathan whose son he is and calling him to filial loyalty to the royal house. | He explains the phrase through kinship and sexual idiom: the mother's nakedness points to Saul's conjugal relationship with Jonathan's mother. Saul is shaming Jonathan by saying that siding with David dishonors his own birth, parentage, and obligations as the king's son. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=13836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:36</a> | Why does 1 Samuel 21:4 ask whether the young men had kept themselves from women? | He says campaign purity mattered for soldiers, and abstinence plus ritual cleanliness were relevant before eating consecrated bread. | He identifies the young men as David's troops and connects the question to Israelite military purity. Soldiers on campaign were expected to abstain, as in the Uriah example, and that ritual cleanliness mattered before they could receive consecrated bread. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=14017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:37</a> | Is there an Egyptian cognate behind the divine-name language in Exodus, such as a root like `yah`? | He says no: Egyptian grammar does not support that proposal, and he rejects the claimed parallels. | He rejects the proposed Egyptian root and says the verb-to-be claim is not Egyptian grammar. He treats the suggested "yah" parallel and related "I am" wording as a mistaken attempt to manufacture an Egyptian cognate for the biblical divine-name language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=14322" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:42</a> | What are your thoughts on Brian Young's work? | He gives a strongly negative answer, calling Young unqualified and saying his teaching repeats badly mistaken fringe Egyptology. | He says he had reviewed a substantial portion of Young's material and found the Egyptology deeply incompetent. His criticism centers on Young distributing teaching materials despite poor qualifications, repeated errors, and fringe-style misunderstandings of Egyptian language and history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/nD2dx7zHZ80?t=16260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:31:00</a> | As a Catholic Egyptology master's student, what advice do you have if most of academia is not religious? | He advises keeping religion private in the academy, choosing religiously neutral programs, and approaching the university as a place to learn rather than preach. | He advises the student not to proselytize or foreground religion in the academic setting, especially before finishing the degree. His practical counsel is to keep faith private at school, select programs where religion is not the point of conflict, and treat the academy as a place to learn the discipline. |
